examples.title: Circular Array Deque Implementation
examples.description: Complete implementation of deque operations using circular array with O(1) time complexity for all operations
examples.code:
CLASS Deque:
  CONSTANT MAXSIZE = 100
  ARRAY arr[MAXSIZE]
  INTEGER front = -1
  INTEGER rear = -1

  FUNCTION isEmpty():
    RETURN (front == -1)

  FUNCTION isFull():
    RETURN ((rear + 1) % MAXSIZE == front)

  FUNCTION insertFront(x):
    IF isFull() THEN:
      PRINT "Overflow"
      RETURN
    IF isEmpty() THEN:
      front = 0
      rear = 0
    ELSE:
      front = (front - 1 + MAXSIZE) % MAXSIZE
    arr[front] = x

  FUNCTION insertRear(x):
    IF isFull() THEN:
      PRINT "Overflow"
      RETURN
    IF isEmpty() THEN:
      front = 0
      rear = 0
    ELSE:
      rear = (rear + 1) % MAXSIZE
    arr[rear] = x

  FUNCTION deleteFront():
    IF isEmpty() THEN:
      PRINT "Underflow"
      RETURN NULL
    element = arr[front]
    IF front == rear THEN:
      front = -1
      rear = -1
    ELSE:
      front = (front + 1) % MAXSIZE
    RETURN element

  FUNCTION deleteRear():
    IF isEmpty() THEN:
      PRINT "Underflow"
      RETURN NULL
    element = arr[rear]
    IF front == rear THEN:
      front = -1
      rear = -1
    ELSE:
      rear = (rear - 1 + MAXSIZE) % MAXSIZE
    RETURN element

  FUNCTION getFront():
    IF isEmpty() THEN RETURN NULL
    RETURN arr[front]

  FUNCTION getRear():
    IF isEmpty() THEN RETURN NULL
    RETURN arr[rear]
examples.explanation: This circular array implementation achieves O(1) for all operations by using modulo arithmetic for wrap-around. The key insight is that we use n-1 positions to distinguish full from empty: when (rear+1)%n == front, the deque is full. The front and rear pointers wrap around using the formula (index ± 1 + n) % n.

examples.title: Doubly Linked List Deque Implementation
examples.description: Linked-list based deque implementation with head and tail pointers for O(1) operations
examples.code:
CLASS Node:
  DATA value
  Node prev
  Node next

CLASS LinkedDeque:
  Node head = NULL
  Node tail = NULL

  FUNCTION isEmpty():
    RETURN (head == NULL)

  FUNCTION insertFront(x):
    newNode = Node(x)
    newNode.next = head
    IF NOT isEmpty() THEN:
      head.prev = newNode
    ELSE:
      tail = newNode
    head = newNode

  FUNCTION insertRear(x):
    newNode = Node(x)
    newNode.prev = tail
    IF NOT isEmpty() THEN:
      tail.next = newNode
    ELSE:
      head = newNode
    tail = newNode

  FUNCTION deleteFront():
    IF isEmpty() THEN:
      PRINT "Underflow"
      RETURN NULL
    element = head.value
    head = head.next
    IF head != NULL THEN:
      head.prev = NULL
    ELSE:
      tail = NULL
    RETURN element

  FUNCTION deleteRear():
    IF isEmpty() THEN:
      PRINT "Underflow"
      RETURN NULL
    element = tail.value
    tail = tail.prev
    IF tail != NULL THEN:
      tail.next = NULL
    ELSE:
      head = NULL
    RETURN element

  FUNCTION getFront():
    IF isEmpty() THEN RETURN NULL
    RETURN head.value

  FUNCTION getRear():
    IF isEmpty() THEN RETURN NULL
    RETURN tail.value
examples.explanation: This doubly linked list implementation maintains both head and tail pointers, enabling O(1) access to both ends. Each operation involves updating at most 3-4 pointers: the new/existing node's pointers and the head/tail references. No traversal is needed.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for n elements, plus O(1) for pointers

examples.title: Dynamic Array Deque with Geometric Resizing
examples.description: Deque implementation that dynamically resizes when full, achieving amortized O(1) insertion
examples.code:
CLASS DynamicDeque:
  ARRAY arr
  INTEGER capacity = 4
  INTEGER front = 0
  INTEGER rear = 0
  INTEGER size = 0

  FUNCTION resize(newCap):
    newArr = ARRAY(newCap)
    FOR i = 0 TO size-1:
      newArr[i] = arr[(front + i) % capacity]
    arr = newArr
    front = 0
    rear = size
    capacity = newCap

  FUNCTION insertRear(x):
    IF size == capacity THEN:
      resize(2 * capacity)
    arr[rear] = x
    rear = (rear + 1) % capacity
    size = size + 1

  FUNCTION deleteFront():
    IF size == 0 THEN:
      PRINT "Underflow"
      RETURN NULL
    element = arr[front]
    front = (front + 1) % capacity
    size = size - 1
    IF size > 0 AND size == capacity / 4 THEN:
      resize(capacity / 2)
    RETURN element

  FUNCTION insertFront(x):
    IF size == capacity THEN:
      resize(2 * capacity)
    front = (front - 1 + capacity) % capacity
    arr[front] = x
    size = size + 1

  FUNCTION deleteRear():
    IF size == 0 THEN:
      PRINT "Underflow"
      RETURN NULL
    rear = (rear - 1 + capacity) % capacity
    element = arr[rear]
    size = size - 1
    IF size > 0 AND size == capacity / 4 THEN:
      resize(capacity / 2)
    RETURN element
examples.explanation: This implementation uses geometric resizing (doubling on overflow, halving when quarter-full). The amortized cost of n insertions is O(1) because each element is copied at most once during doubling phases, and the total cost of n operations sums to O(n).
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is current number of elements
